mappings.rationaleAI: Surveys the development of rational inquiry from Presocratic cosmology through Socrates, Plato, and Aristotle (A.1.b-d), Hellenistic scholarship (A.1.e), Scholasticism (A.2.d), Renaissance humanism (A.3.a-b), and Enlightenment through modern philosophy (A.3.d-h). Non-Western coverage includes Indian, Chinese, and Japanese traditions (A.6) and Islamic Kalam (A.5).

Art as a domain encompassing visual arts, theatre, music, literature, and aesthetics, with its contested definition and historical evolution from craft to fine art, connects to diverse views of the definition and scope of the humanities (B.1).
Socrates' turn from cosmology to moral inquiry through dialectical questioning (the Socratic method) represents the Sophists and Socrates' turn to logos and the beginnings of rhetoric (A.1.b).

The Islamic Golden Age's intensive intellectual production in philosophy, theology, law, literature, and science across Baghdad, Cordoba, and Cairo represents the Islamic tradition of humanistic scholarship and its methods and development (A.5).

The Renaissance's recovery of classical antiquity, humanism, vernacular literature (Dante, Petrarch), and the rise of the free individual directly maps to the idea of Renaissance, the ideal of the classical, and the concept of the dignity of the individual (A.3.a), as well as humanism and the new learning (A.3.b).

The Enlightenment's separation of science from philosophy, its dissemination through encyclopedias and the print trade, and the quarrel between ancients and moderns connect to the separation of science from philosophy (A.3.d) and the problem of progress in learning (A.3.e).

Aristotle's founding of the Lyceum, invention of logic (the Organon), division and organization of the sciences, and systematic works on natural philosophy, ethics, and politics directly map to Aristotle and the Lyceum (A.1.d).

Philosophy as the parent discipline of the natural and social sciences, with branches including epistemology, ethics, logic, and metaphysics, connects to the beginnings of learning (A.1), the distinction of philosophy from sacred theology (A.2.e), and theories of the humanities as a fundamental division of knowledge (B.3).

Ancient Greek civilization's philosophy (Socrates, Plato, Aristotle), literature, and mathematics represent the beginnings of learning and the ideal of paideia (A.1), from Homeric education through the Hellenistic period.

Education's history from ancient oral transmission through medieval institutions to modern universal schooling connects to Homeric education (A.1.a), the founding of the universities (A.2.c), the rise of the liberal arts college and graduate school (A.3.g), and the humanities as an educational program (B.2).

Plato's founding of the Academy, his Theory of Forms, and his dialogues on politics, ethics, and metaphysics directly map to Plato and the Academy, including the relation of theology to mythology and mathematics in the service of philosophy (A.1.c).
Homer's Iliad and Odyssey as foundational epics embodying the ideal of the hero, heroic honor, and fate directly constitute Homeric education and the ideal of the hero (A.1.a).

Dante's Divine Comedy in vernacular Tuscan and his defense of the vernacular (De Vulgari Eloquentia) directly connect to the idea of Renaissance, the rise of the vernacular, and the concept of the dignity of the free individual (A.3.a).

The cognitive capacity for logical reasoning and the tension between reason and faith connect to the conflict of cultural ideals among rhetoric, philosophy, and science (A.1.g), the distinction of philosophy from sacred theology (A.2.e), and the question of distinguishing humanities from sciences (B.3).

Visual arts including painting, sculpture, photography, and architecture, with their historical development and aesthetic theory, connect to diverse views of the definition and scope of the humanities (B.1).

Aquinas' synthesis of Aristotelian philosophy with Catholic doctrine and his Summa Theologiae directly represent the Scholastic method (A.2.d) and the distinction of philosophy from sacred theology through faith-reason reconciliation (A.2.e).

Descartes' method of radical doubt and foundationalism represent the search for a universal method (A.3.c), and his grounding of knowledge in individual rational inquiry rather than authority connects to the separation of science from philosophy (A.3.d).

The University of Bologna (1088) and the evolution of universities from Church-sponsored cathedral schools directly connect to the founding of the universities (A.2.c), the rise of the liberal arts college and graduate school (A.3.g), and the organization of the contemporary university (A.3.h.i).

Science as systematic knowledge-building distinguished from technology, spanning natural, social, and formal sciences, connects to the separation of science from philosophy (A.3.d), the knowledge explosion (A.3.h), and theories of the humanities as a fundamental division of knowledge distinct from the sciences (B.3).

Avicenna's Book of Healing covering logic, natural science, mathematics, and metaphysics, and his Peripatetic philosophy influencing both Islamic theology and European Scholasticism, represents the Islamic tradition of humanistic scholarship and its relation to classical and Christian learning (A.5).
Galileo's experimental method, championing of heliocentrism, and conflict with the Roman Inquisition represent the separation of science from philosophy and the rise of empirical science (A.3.d).
Newton's Principia Mathematica establishing classical mechanics represents the rise and development of empirical science as distinct from philosophy (A.3.d).

The history of science tracing empirical inquiry from ancient philosophy through the Scientific Revolution and institutional history connects to the separation of science from philosophy (A.3.d) and the growth and proliferation of special disciplines (A.3.h).

The Middle Ages' feudalism, Church and monasticism, scholasticism and universities, and the Crusades directly map to Christian learning in antiquity and the Middle Ages (A.2), including the codification of liberal arts and the founding of universities.

The novel's history from Cervantes through 18th-century British fiction and parallel East Asian traditions connects to the rise of the vernacular (A.3.a) and represents a humanistic literary form within the scope of the humanities (B.1).

Literature spanning fiction, poetry, and drama, with its academic study through literary criticism and textual criticism, connects to the rise of the vernacular (A.3.a), the growth of modern humanistic scholarship from belles lettres to scientific investigation (A.3.f), and the definition and scope of the humanities (B.1).

Alexander's conquests triggering the Hellenistic period, during which Greek culture spread across the Near East and cultural centers like Alexandria flourished, connects to Hellenistic scholarship and Alexandria as a cultural centre (A.1.e).

Leonardo's systematic empirical curiosity spanning arts and sciences simultaneously represents the ideal of the classical and the concept of the dignity of the free individual in the Renaissance (A.3.a) and humanism and the new learning (A.3.b).

Cicero as Rome's preeminent rhetorical theorist and philosopher who adapted Hellenistic schools into Latin directly represents the Roman ideal of humanitas and the training of the orator (A.1.f) and the conflict among rhetoric, philosophy, and science (A.1.g).

Archaeology as the systematic study of human activity through material culture connects to the growth of modern humanistic scholarship through the scientific investigation of antiquity via archaeology and philology (A.3.f).

The history of art surveying aesthetic production across civilizations, combining formal, iconographic, and cultural analysis, connects to the growth of modern humanistic scholarship (A.3.f) and diverse views of the definition and scope of the humanities (B.1).

The history of literature tracing narrative, poetry, and prose across civilizations connects to the rise of the vernacular (A.3.a), the growth of modern humanistic scholarship (A.3.f), and the scope of the humanities (B.1).

The Scientific Revolution's departure from Aristotelian and scholastic frameworks through heliocentrism, empiricism, and mathematization of nature directly represents the separation of science from philosophy (A.3.d).

Epistemology's examination of knowledge sources and limits including empiricism, rationalism, and foundationalism connects to the search for a universal method (A.3.c) and the question of distinguishing the humanities from the sciences (B.3).

Western philosophy from pre-Socratic inquiry through Scholasticism, Renaissance humanism, and modern traditions maps comprehensively across the beginnings of learning (A.1), Christian learning (A.2), and the development of humane letters from the Renaissance to the present (A.3).

Mahayana Buddhism's philosophical traditions including Madhyamaka emptiness philosophy, Yogacara consciousness doctrine, and the bodhisattva ideal represent aspects of humanities and humanistic scholarship in the East, particularly in India, China, and Japan (A.6).

The scientific method's empirical framework with hypothesis formation, experimentation, and falsifiability connects to the search for a universal method (A.3.c) and the separation of science from philosophy through the rise of empirical science (A.3.d).
The umbrella category of the arts encompassing visual, literary, and performing arts connects to diverse views of the definition and scope of the humanities (B.1).

History as an academic discipline with debates over whether it is a social science, humanity, or hybrid connects to the growth of humanistic scholarship (A.3.f), the proliferation of special disciplines (A.3.h), the scope of the humanities (B.1), and the question of distinguishing humanities from sciences (B.3).

Philosophical analysis of knowledge including epistemology, foundationalism, and the value of knowledge connects to the search for a universal method (A.3.c) and theories of the humanities as a fundamental division of knowledge (B.3).

Logic as the study of correct reasoning from Aristotelian syllogistics through Frege connects to Aristotle's invention of logic at the Lyceum (A.1.d) and the Scholastic method with its emphasis on logic (A.2.d).
